task_prompt: A regional grocery chain trained a gradient-boosted demand forecasting model for weekly unit sales across 600 store-SKU pairs. The model uses three years of history and predicts one week ahead. Inputs include lagged sales, holiday indicators, local weather, planned promotions, and whether an item is temporarily displayed on an endcap. The team evaluates on the most recent 20 weeks and reports weighted absolute percentage error of 11.8%, compared with 18.9% for the baseline. The baseline is described as the retailer’s current seasonal heuristic: forecast next week’s sales using sales from the same week last year. The paper emphasizes that the new model is particularly strong around holidays and promotion periods, where the error gap widens further. Buried in the methods section, the authors state that the heuristic baseline does not ingest the promotion calendar or endcap schedule even though those plans are available before forecast time and are part of the model inputs. They also mention that the retailer’s current heuristic was designed for categories with relatively stable pricing and was never re-parameterized after the chain expanded from 18 to 34 stores, increasing promotion frequency. The authors argue that this is the operational baseline and therefore the relevant benchmark. They additionally report that all model features are known before the week begins and that prediction latency is negligible because forecasts are computed overnight. The operations team wants to use the reported results to justify replacing the chain’s replenishment planning logic before the next holiday season.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The comparison is unfair because the heuristic baseline was denied available promotion information and left unrefreshed despite a major operating-regime change.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Compare against a re-estimated statistical baseline that receives the same known-ahead promotion and display inputs, such as dynamic regression or ETS with exogenous regressors.

planted_issues.description: The baseline ignores promotion and endcap plans that are available before forecast time and provided to the new model, creating an asymmetric information set.
The retailer expanded stores and promotion frequency changed, but the baseline heuristic was not updated to reflect the new operating regime.
The study omits a reasonable statistical benchmark with exogenous variables, so it cannot isolate whether ML beats a competent non-ML approach.

ideal_critique: The model gets planned promotion information that the baseline is not allowed to use even though it is known at prediction time.
A stale seasonal heuristic is an operational comparator, not a fair scientific baseline.
A dynamic regression or ETSX baseline would better test whether complex ML is needed.
ideal_defense: Comparing with the chain’s current process is useful for a business replacement decision.
Using only known-ahead features is methodologically correct.
Holiday-period gains may still persist even against a stronger baseline, but that was not shown.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: An exogenous-input statistical baseline reduces the WAPE gap to under 3 points.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A competent exogenous baseline still trails by at least 6 WAPE points across both normal and holiday weeks.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The new model stays better overall, but the holiday-period advantage shrinks materially once the baseline is upgraded.

must_not_claim: Weather should never be used in grocery forecasting
WAPE is automatically invalid for this use case

ground_truth_rationale: The baseline is broken because it both lacks available inputs and has not been refreshed after a meaningful business change. The reported gap therefore overstates the evidence that the ML model is intrinsically better.
notes: Easy case. Red herring: the weather feature may attract unnecessary criticism, but it is perfectly valid because it is known before ordering.

task_prompt: A document AI startup developed a vision-language model to extract totals, merchant names, dates, and tax amounts from retail receipts. The evaluation set contains 8,400 scanned receipts from restaurants, supermarkets, gas stations, and pharmacies collected from users of a bookkeeping app. The startup compares its system against a commercial OCR product that many small customers already use. On exact-field accuracy, the new model scores 91.4% while the commercial product scores 83.0%. The startup highlights that its model also handles rotated images and crumpled paper better than the commercial engine. In the experimental details, the commercial OCR service was run with its default general-document configuration because the team did not have access to the optional receipt-specific parser included in the vendor’s enterprise tier. The startup also notes that around 30% of the evaluation set consists of Canadian receipts with bilingual English-French text and taxes listed in province-specific formats. The commercial product was executed with its default English setting, while the startup fine-tuned its model on both English and French receipts. The authors say this still reflects a realistic small-business deployment because many buyers use the lower-cost default package. They add that their model’s median processing time is 1.7 seconds per image, compared with 0.6 seconds for the commercial product, which they believe is acceptable for bookkeeping workflows. They plan to present the study to prospective customers as evidence that their model clearly outperforms incumbent OCR solutions.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The new model may well be stronger, but the comparison handicaps the commercial baseline by denying receipt-specific parsing and bilingual configuration that are directly relevant to this dataset.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Re-run the vendor baseline with the receipt parser and bilingual settings, then compare field-level accuracy and latency on the same receipt mix.

planted_issues.description: The commercial baseline was run in a generic default mode instead of its receipt-specific configuration, so the comparison does not reflect the vendor’s best applicable setup.
The evaluation includes bilingual Canadian receipts, but the commercial baseline was run only in English while the new model was trained for both languages.
The study emphasizes accuracy without fully discussing whether the slower model remains preferable once throughput and user experience are considered.

ideal_critique: A commercial OCR system should be evaluated in the configuration that matches the document type being studied.
Running English-only OCR on a bilingual receipt set is an avoidable handicap.
The latency difference does not invalidate the model, but it should be part of the comparison if the claim is market superiority.
ideal_defense: Default-package performance is operationally relevant for price-sensitive customers.
The new model may still retain an advantage even after a fairer vendor configuration is used.
Receipt rotation robustness is a legitimate value-add and not merely a benchmark artifact.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The vendor baseline with receipt parsing and bilingual settings closes the field-accuracy gap to under 3 points.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The new model still leads by more than 5 points after the vendor is configured appropriately, with acceptable latency for the target workflow.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Accuracy remains somewhat better for the new model, but the advantage becomes small enough that price and speed determine the practical choice.

must_not_claim: The evaluation set is too small to say anything
Any use of fine-tuning makes the comparison unfair by itself

ground_truth_rationale: This case is mixed because the model’s gain could be real, but the paper did not compare against the strongest applicable baseline configuration. A careful review should question the baseline without claiming the new model is necessarily weak.
notes: Easy mixed case. Red herring: slower processing time looks problematic, but in bookkeeping workflows it may still be operationally acceptable.

task_prompt: An academic hospital built a gradient-boosted model to predict sepsis six hours before clinical recognition in emergency department patients. The dataset contains 42,000 encounters over four years. The model updates hourly using the latest vitals, laboratory results, and nurse-charted observations. It is compared against qSOFA, a widely known three-variable bedside score. The paper reports AUROC of 0.86 for the new model versus 0.71 for qSOFA and claims a major advance in early sepsis recognition. The model prediction is evaluated using the best score available during the six hours before recognition. For qSOFA, however, the authors compute the score only once, using the first complete set of vital signs recorded within 30 minutes of emergency department arrival. In a supplementary note, they explain this reflects how qSOFA is commonly documented in their institution’s triage workflow. They also note that lactate and white-blood-cell measurements are not always available at arrival but usually become available within the first few hours, which benefits the hourly-updating ML model. The paper argues that the comparison is clinically meaningful because clinicians make early triage decisions near arrival. Still, the system is proposed for deployment as a continuously updating sepsis surveillance tool, not only as a triage screen. The authors additionally report that the model’s median lead time among true positives is 3.1 hours and that all features were extracted from routine EHR data already present in the hospital system. The study is being used to support a request for a prospective silent trial.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The baseline is broken because qSOFA is evaluated as a one-time arrival score while the model is evaluated as a continuously updating six-hour surveillance system with more information over time.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Recompute qSOFA as an hourly-updating comparator over the same pre-recognition window, or compare the model against an arrival-only baseline using only arrival-time features.

planted_issues.description: The new model is allowed to update across the six hours before recognition, but qSOFA is frozen at arrival, creating an unfair temporal-information advantage.
The model benefits from labs that are often unavailable at arrival, so the comparison does not isolate modeling quality from later information access.
The authors justify the baseline using triage practice even though the deployment claim is for ongoing surveillance, which is a different use case.

ideal_critique: A continuously updating sepsis monitor should be compared with a continuously updating baseline, not an arrival-only score.
Some of the apparent gain may come from later lab availability rather than superior modeling.
The paper switches between a triage framing and a surveillance framing to defend the comparator.
ideal_defense: qSOFA is a clinically familiar comparator and is relevant if the intended decision point were arrival triage.
Using routinely collected EHR features is a genuine strength.
The model’s lead-time result could still be valuable even if the baseline needs to be reframed.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: An hourly-updating qSOFA or NEWS-style comparator substantially reduces the AUROC or lead-time gap.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The model remains clearly better after both systems are evaluated on the same information horizon.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The gap persists on discrimination but shrinks enough that implementation burden becomes the main question.

must_not_claim: Gradient boosting cannot be used in clinical settings
AUROC is automatically the wrong metric for sepsis
Using routine EHR data is itself suspicious

ground_truth_rationale: The issue is not whether qSOFA is famous or whether the model is promising. It is that the comparator is evaluated at a different temporal horizon, so the study confounds information timing with model quality.
notes: Medium case requiring some clinical workflow understanding. Red herring: prospective silent-trial planning is reasonable and does not repair the comparator problem.

task_prompt: A payments company built a graph neural network to detect account-takeover fraud. The model uses transaction links, shared devices, merchant overlap, and recent password-reset activity. It is trained on nine months of transactions and evaluated on the tenth month. The paper compares the graph model against the company’s existing rules engine and reports that at a manual-review rate of 0.8% of transactions, the graph model captures 78% of confirmed fraud versus 63% for the rules engine. The authors describe this as a large operational gain. In the methods section, the graph model’s alert threshold is explicitly tuned on a validation set to hit the target review-rate budget of 0.8%. The rules engine, by contrast, is evaluated at its production threshold, which historically targeted about 1.2% review volume but happened to yield 0.8% review volume on the specific test month because transaction volumes were seasonally low. The appendix also notes that fraud analysts revised many rule weights four months before the study, but those revised weights were not replayed because only the production snapshot at deployment start was archived. The company argues that replaying the live rules engine is the only practical benchmark and most relevant for a replacement decision. They further report that the graph model met nightly batch latency constraints and that feature construction excluded any investigator notes not available at score time. The risk team wants to use the result to justify redirecting analyst headcount toward the new system.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The graph model may be stronger, but the baseline comparison is weakened because the rules engine is evaluated at a stale archived configuration and not explicitly tuned to the same operating point.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Replay the latest available rules configuration and retune both systems to the same validation-defined review budget before comparing fraud capture on the held-out month.

planted_issues.description: The graph model is tuned to the target review budget while the rules engine is evaluated at a production threshold that only coincidentally lands near that budget on the test month.
The replayed rules engine uses an older archived snapshot rather than the revised weights used more recently by analysts, overstating the gap if rules improved before the test period.
The study supports potential replacement consideration, but not a clean estimate of architecture advantage over a current properly tuned rule system.

ideal_critique: Alerting systems should be compared at explicitly matched operating points, not approximately similar realized review rates.
A stale archived rules snapshot is not a fair estimate of the current comparator.
The paper partly measures model quality and partly measures benchmark maintenance quality.
ideal_defense: Replay of the actual production system is operationally relevant for a replacement decision.
The graph model’s features appear to be score-time legal and the batch pipeline constraint is handled correctly.
Even after a fairer replay, the graph model may still retain a real lead.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Once both systems are retuned to the same validation-defined review budget, the fraud-capture gap shrinks to under 5 points.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A refreshed and budget-matched rules engine still trails the graph model by more than 10 fraud-capture points.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The graph model stays ahead, but a nontrivial share of the original advantage disappears after refreshing the rules baseline.

must_not_claim: Graph neural networks are inherently too opaque for fraud
Nightly batch scoring is unrealistic by itself
Using password-reset activity must be leakage

ground_truth_rationale: This case is mixed because the model could truly outperform the rules engine, but the paper did not construct the cleanest comparator. A careful judge should question the replay setup without concluding the graph model has no value.
notes: Medium mixed case. Red herring: password-reset activity sounds leakage-prone, but it is explicitly available before scoring and is a legitimate fraud feature.

task_prompt: A music streaming service developed a two-stage recommendation stack with a neural retrieval model followed by a gradient-boosted ranker. The offline study focuses on the retrieval stage. The new retriever uses user listening history, artist embeddings, skip behavior, and audio-content embeddings for tracks. It is compared against matrix factorization trained on historical play events. On full-catalog Recall@100, the neural retriever scores 0.43 versus 0.31 for matrix factorization. The paper argues this demonstrates a clear improvement in candidate generation quality. In the experimental details, the neural retriever is trained only on impressions from the current home-screen product, where every impression includes a logged exposure indicator. The matrix-factorization baseline is trained on the service’s legacy play-only table from a wider date range because the authors say reconstructing impression-level negatives for the baseline pipeline would have been too expensive. The new model therefore benefits from skip labels and explicit non-consumption signals, whereas the baseline is trained only on positive play events. The paper also notes that audio-content embeddings are especially helpful for new tracks and reports a cold-start Recall@100 gain of 19 points. The authors say the comparison is reasonable because matrix factorization is the incumbent retrieval method in production. They add that both systems meet retrieval-latency constraints and that the next step is a full online ranking experiment. Internal reviewers ask whether the result isolates neural retrieval quality or mostly reflects richer supervision and content features provided only to the new system.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The baseline is broken because it is trained on a weaker supervision source and denied content features that are central to the new model’s advantage, so the study does not isolate retrieval architecture quality.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Train the baseline on the same impression-level data if possible, and add a content-aware non-neural baseline before claiming architectural superiority.

planted_issues.description: The new retriever uses impression-level positives and negatives while the baseline is trained only on play events, creating an unequal learning problem.
Audio-content embeddings are available only to the new model, so the reported gain conflates architecture improvements with richer features.
The study omits a simpler content-aware retrieval baseline that could test whether the gain requires a neural architecture.

ideal_critique: A fair retrieval comparison should give both systems the same supervision source and comparable features.
Cold-start gains are unsurprising if only one model receives content embeddings.
The study supports replacing a legacy pipeline, but not a clean claim about architectural superiority.
ideal_defense: Comparing with the actual production retriever is relevant for product decisions.
Full-catalog evaluation and latency reporting are good methodological practices.
Even after stronger baselines are added, the neural retriever may still be best.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A content-aware or impression-trained baseline closes most of the Recall@100 gap.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Under matched supervision and feature access, the neural retriever still retains a large recall advantage.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The gap persists overall but the cold-start advantage largely disappears once features are matched.

must_not_claim: Full-catalog Recall@100 is an invalid offline metric by itself
Matrix factorization can never use negatives

ground_truth_rationale: The main issue is baseline construction: one system is learning from richer supervision and richer features. That makes the benchmark operationally interesting but methodologically weak as evidence for architectural superiority.
notes: Medium case. Red herring: the strong cold-start result looks compelling, but because feature access differs it cannot by itself prove the architecture is better.

task_prompt: A quantitative energy-trading team developed a transformer model to forecast next-day hourly wholesale electricity prices in a deregulated market. The model uses five years of historical prices, unit outages, load forecasts, wind forecasts, gas hub prices, and interconnector schedules. It is evaluated on the most recent six months after a major market-rule redesign that introduced new scarcity-pricing behavior during reserve shortfalls. The paper compares the transformer against an ARIMA baseline and reports mean absolute error of $7.80 per MWh for the transformer versus $11.60 for ARIMA. The authors argue that this proves deep learning is clearly superior for short-term price formation. In the methods appendix, the ARIMA baseline is fit once on a pre-redesign training window ending just before the market-rule change and then frozen for the entire six-month test period. It also uses only lagged prices, because the authors say adding exogenous regressors to the old ARIMA codebase would have taken extra engineering effort. The transformer is retrained monthly on rolling data and receives the exogenous fuel, weather, and outage inputs. The paper notes that traders care most about the top 5% of high-price hours because hedging losses are concentrated there, but the headline evaluation remains overall MAE. The authors say the benchmark is fair because ARIMA represents the firm’s legacy model and the objective was to assess whether a modern deep model is a better production replacement. They plan to use the result to justify a capital allocation change for next summer.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The baseline is broken because the legacy ARIMA is both frozen across a market-regime change and denied exogenous drivers that are central to price formation, so the reported gap cannot be attributed cleanly to model class.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Re-estimate a rolling ARIMAX or other strong statistical baseline on post-redesign data with the same known-ahead exogenous inputs, and compare tail-hour and overall performance separately.

planted_issues.description: The ARIMA baseline is fit before a market-rule redesign and then frozen through the test period, so it is structurally stale in a changed price regime.
The transformer gets outages, load, wind, gas, and interconnector schedules while the ARIMA baseline only uses lagged prices, confounding architecture with input richness.
Overall MAE understates the hours traders care about most, but that is secondary to the broken baseline comparison.

ideal_critique: A frozen pre-redesign ARIMA is not a fair comparator in a post-redesign market.
Known-ahead exogenous drivers are crucial in power-price forecasting, so the input mismatch matters greatly.
The study partly measures modernization of the data pipeline, not just transformer superiority.
ideal_defense: Comparing with the actual legacy production model is relevant for a replacement decision.
Rolling retraining and exogenous inputs are legitimate for the proposed production system.
The transformer may still win against a fair ARIMAX baseline, but the current evidence does not show that.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A rolling ARIMAX substantially reduces the MAE gap or matches the transformer on high-price hours.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Even with rolling refits and matched exogenous drivers, strong statistical baselines still underperform materially.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The transformer remains better overall, but the headline advantage shrinks and tail-risk performance becomes the deciding factor.

must_not_claim: Transformers can never be used for time series
Using gas prices always causes leakage

ground_truth_rationale: This hard case has two simultaneous baseline failures: the comparator is stale after a rule change and it receives much less information. In electricity markets, both choices materially distort the size of any apparent deep-learning advantage.
notes: Hard domain-specific case in energy forecasting. Red herring: the transformer architecture may provoke generic skepticism, but the real issue is the comparator setup.

task_prompt: A genomics startup trained a deep sequence model to predict whether missense variants in an inherited cardiomyopathy panel are pathogenic. The benchmark contains 18,000 variants with ClinVar labels curated up to a fixed cutoff date. The model ingests amino-acid context, conservation features, and protein-language-model embeddings. It is compared against PolyPhen-2, a widely used legacy pathogenicity score, and achieves AUROC of 0.93 versus 0.84 for PolyPhen-2. The paper frames this as strong evidence that modern foundation-model representations outperform classical variant-effect predictors. In the evaluation details, the new model is calibrated and thresholded only on missense variants from cardiomyopathy genes, while PolyPhen-2 is used off the shelf with its generic published threshold. The authors also note that approximately 40% of the evaluation set comes from two sarcomere genes with unusually well-characterized pathogenic hotspots. A supplementary table indicates that the startup excluded variants of uncertain significance from the benchmark and retained only binary pathogenic or benign labels after curator review. The company argues that using the default PolyPhen-2 threshold is fair because clinicians often consume that score as-is in tertiary analysis pipelines. They further mention that the proposed model is intended as a gene-panel-specific decision support tool rather than a genome-wide classifier. Based on the reported AUROC gap, they suggest the new score should replace older pathogenicity tools in inherited-cardiac variant review workflows.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The baseline is broken because a gene-panel-specific calibrated model is being compared against an untuned generic legacy score, and hotspot-heavy evaluation genes further favor the specialized system.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Calibrate the legacy baseline on the same cardiomyopathy panel, evaluate per-gene performance, and compare against other modern variant-effect predictors before claiming model-class superiority.

planted_issues.description: The new model is calibrated for a specific cardiomyopathy panel, while PolyPhen-2 is used with a generic threshold, making the comparison asymmetric.
A large fraction of the benchmark comes from well-characterized hotspot genes, which can exaggerate apparent gains for a panel-specific model.
Comparing only to one legacy tool is insufficient to support a broad claim about modern foundation-model methods outperforming classical or recent alternatives.

ideal_critique: A specialized calibrated predictor should not be compared only to a generic off-the-shelf thresholded legacy score.
Per-gene composition matters in variant interpretation because hotspot genes are easier than under-characterized genes.
The paper supports panel-specific utility at most, not a broad methodological victory claim.
ideal_defense: A panel-specific tool is clinically relevant if that is the intended deployment context.
Using fixed cutoff-date ClinVar labels and excluding uncertain labels is a defensible benchmark construction choice.
The model may still be genuinely superior, but stronger matched baselines are required.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A panel-calibrated legacy or recent non-deep baseline narrows the AUROC gap substantially, especially outside hotspot genes.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Across per-gene analyses and calibrated comparators, the new model still shows a robust advantage.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The advantage holds in hotspot genes but not in the rarer, less-characterized cardiomyopathy genes.

must_not_claim: ClinVar labels are unusable for benchmarking
Protein language model embeddings are inherently invalid
Excluding uncertain labels is automatically wrong

ground_truth_rationale: This case requires domain knowledge about variant interpretation workflows: gene-panel calibration and gene composition strongly affect benchmark difficulty. The result may indicate useful clinical specialization, but it does not justify the broad superiority claim made from this baseline.
notes: Hard domain-specific case in clinical genomics. Red herring: excluding variants of uncertain significance may look suspicious, but for a binary benchmark it is a defensible simplification.

task_prompt: A radiology AI company trained a 3D convolutional model to triage follow-up chest CT scans for pulmonary nodule growth. The evaluation set contains 2,300 studies from a cancer-screening program, and the endpoint is whether a study should be escalated for expedited radiologist review because a previously seen nodule shows interval growth. The model processes the full 3D CT volume plus two prior exams when available. It is compared with a human baseline composed of four general radiologists who each reviewed a subset of cases in a reader study. The paper reports sensitivity of 0.91 at 0.80 specificity for the model versus mean reader sensitivity of 0.76 at 0.81 specificity. In the reader-study protocol, radiologists received the current CT scan and a brief indication that the patient was in a screening program, but prior exams were not shown because the reader-study software did not support synchronized prior-view display. The authors state that prior exams were omitted for humans to standardize the interface and keep reading time manageable. They also note that one of the four readers was a thoracic specialist but the others were general radiologists covering mixed modalities. In routine clinical practice, thoracic radiologists often review prior CTs and dictation history when deciding whether apparent growth is real or due to slice positioning. The company argues that the result still demonstrates superhuman performance because the average human reader under the study protocol was substantially worse than the model. They further report that the model’s inference time is under 30 seconds per study and that the intended deployment is triage assistance rather than autonomous diagnosis.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model may be useful, but the human baseline is unfairly weakened because readers were denied prior exams and specialist context that are central to assessing interval growth on follow-up CT.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Repeat the reader study with prior CTs and prior reports available, ideally with thoracic radiologists or a matched specialist mix, then compare at the same operating point.

planted_issues.description: Readers were not shown prior CT exams even though interval growth assessment inherently depends on longitudinal comparison, handicapping the human baseline.
The human baseline averages mostly general radiologists for a task often handled with thoracic expertise, weakening the relevance of the comparison.
The paper claims superhuman performance even though the reader study does not reflect normal clinical resources for the human comparator.

ideal_critique: Interval-growth triage is intrinsically longitudinal, so removing prior exams from readers is a major handicap.
A specialist mix matters because thoracic radiologists handle this task differently from generalists.
The evidence may support an assistive tool, but not a strong superhuman claim under routine clinical conditions.
ideal_defense: The model’s assistive triage role could still be valuable even if the human comparison is imperfect.
Matching specificity across readers and model is a reasonable reporting choice.
Operationally, some real workflows also constrain review time, so a fast triage tool remains plausible.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Radiologists with prior exams and thoracic expertise close most of the sensitivity gap at matched specificity.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The model remains clearly superior or complementary even against a properly equipped specialist reader study.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The superiority claim disappears, but the model still improves team performance when used as a second reader.

must_not_claim: Using general radiologists is always invalid

ground_truth_rationale: This is mixed because the model could still be genuinely helpful, but the human baseline is not representative of normal clinical comparison conditions. Longitudinal image review is essential for growth assessment, so the superhuman claim is not established.
notes: Hard domain-specific case in radiology workflow. Red herring: 30-second inference may sound slow, but for triage assistance on CT studies it is not the central methodological concern.

task_prompt: A telecom operator built a churn-risk model to prioritize retention calls. The model scores all prepaid users each week, and the retention team can call at most 2% of the base because agents are limited. Using the prior year of data, the model achieves ROC-AUC of 0.84 on a held-out month, compared with 0.77 for the company’s older logistic model. The paper claims this means the new system will make the retention team significantly more efficient. Features include recent top-up behavior, days since last data usage, complaint history, handset age, and region. The authors note that churn prevalence is about 6% and that the model is recalibrated monthly. They also mention that the call center script and offer menu remained unchanged during the test month. However, the evaluation does not report precision in the top 2%, recall in the top 2%, or incremental retention among contacted users. Instead, the study uses ROC-AUC as the sole headline metric and states that the higher AUC demonstrates better ranking quality overall. A business manager reviewing the results points out that the model will only ever be used to select a very small top slice of users for intervention, and that gains in the middle of the score distribution may not matter at all. The data science team responds that AUC is threshold independent and therefore more robust than any one top-k statistic. They want approval to switch the weekly retention workflow to the new model immediately.

ground_truth.final_verdict: ROC-AUC is informative about global ranking quality, but it is not sufficient for a top-2%-capacity intervention claim where precision-at-k or uplift in the contacted slice is the decision-relevant metric.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Report precision@2%, recall@2%, and ideally retention uplift among contacted users in a live or simulated top-2% intervention policy.

planted_issues.description: ROC-AUC summarizes ranking over all thresholds, but the business action happens only in the top 2% of users, so top-k metrics are more relevant.
The paper does not show model performance under the actual call-center capacity constraint that defines the use case.
Even strong top-k ranking does not prove the calls change churn outcomes; uplift or treatment-effect evidence is absent.

ideal_critique: AUC can improve while top-2% precision stays flat, so the reported gain may not help the call center.
The actual decision is capacity constrained, so evaluation should be too.
Selecting high-risk users is not the same as identifying users who are persuadable by a retention call.
ideal_defense: AUC is a reasonable first-pass ranking metric and the improvement is not meaningless.
Monthly recalibration and stable call scripts are sensible design details.
The model could still be better operationally, but the paper does not yet show it directly.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Precision@2% or uplift among contacted users is unchanged relative to the old model despite the AUC gain.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The new model materially improves retention outcomes or precision within the top 2% slice.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Top-2% precision improves modestly, but evidence on actual persuadability remains weak.

must_not_claim: ROC-AUC should never be reported for churn
The unchanged call script invalidates the test month

ground_truth_rationale: This case is mixed because ROC-AUC is not useless, but it is misaligned with the narrow operational decision. The missing top-k and intervention-relevant metrics are what prevent the deployment claim from being justified.
notes: Easy mixed case. Red herring: recalibration and stable offer scripts are good operational details, not flaws.

task_prompt: A manufacturing team trained a computer-vision classifier to identify cracked ceramic components on an assembly line. The labeled dataset contains 240,000 images, of which about 1.4% contain true defects. The model is a compact convolutional network designed to run on an edge device. On the test set, it reaches 99.1% accuracy compared with 98.6% accuracy for the current inspection rule. The authors highlight the 0.5 percentage point improvement and state that the model is ready to reduce escapes of defective parts to customers. They note that all images were collected from the same camera station over six months and that the edge device can process 45 frames per second, comfortably above line speed. In the discussion section, the quality team explains that the business cost of missing a cracked component is far higher than that of diverting a good part for manual review, because escaped cracks can trigger expensive warranty claims. The paper does not report recall on the defective class, precision at a fixed review rate, or false negatives per million parts. Instead, the headline chart and executive summary focus entirely on overall accuracy and the small improvement over the existing rule. The authors also mention that brightness normalization and minor geometric augmentation were used during training, and that five random seeds produced similar accuracy values. Plant management is using the reported metric to decide whether to replace the current visual rule system with the neural model before a new product launch.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Overall accuracy is the wrong primary metric for this rare-defect inspection claim because it can hide poor recall on the small but costly defective class.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Report defective-class recall, precision, false negatives per million parts, and performance at the manual-review budget relevant to plant operations.

planted_issues.description: With only 1.4% positives, overall accuracy is dominated by non-defective parts and does not show whether the model catches costly cracks.
The business cost is driven by missed defects, but the paper does not report a cost-sensitive or recall-oriented metric.
The paper does not specify performance at the threshold or review burden that would actually be used on the production line.

ideal_critique: A rare-defect inspection system should be judged on missed defects and review burden, not headline accuracy.
The business objective makes false negatives much more important than false positives.
Without an operating point, a 0.5-point accuracy gain is nearly uninterpretable.
ideal_defense: Edge-device throughput and seed stability are real strengths.
Accuracy can still be reported descriptively, but it should not drive the deployment claim.
The model may still be useful once the right metrics are supplied.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Defect recall is low or the model offers no advantage at the false-negative budget acceptable to quality operations.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: At the chosen review burden, the model meaningfully reduces false negatives per million parts while maintaining acceptable throughput.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model improves recall modestly but only with a much higher manual-review load.

must_not_claim: Using data from one camera station automatically invalidates the study
45 frames per second is too slow

ground_truth_rationale: The metric mismatch is direct: the paper claims fewer escaped defects but only reports overall accuracy. In a highly imbalanced inspection problem with asymmetric costs, that evidence is insufficient and potentially misleading.
notes: Easy metric-mismatch case. Red herring: augmentation and seed stability are methodologically fine and do not address the missing recall-oriented evaluation.

task_prompt: A hospital system trained an early-warning model for sepsis to run continuously on inpatient wards. The retrospective dataset covers 180,000 admissions across four hospitals. The paper reports AUROC of 0.88 and highlights that this exceeds the 0.80 AUROC of the incumbent score. Based on this, the authors recommend moving toward bedside deployment. The model produces a risk score every hour. In practice, however, the hospital intends to trigger nurse review whenever the score exceeds a fixed threshold, and nursing leadership has stated that each 30-bed unit can handle at most about two sepsis alerts per shift before alarm fatigue becomes a serious concern. The paper does not report positive predictive value at any threshold, alerts per patient-day, median lead time among true positives at the chosen alert burden, or how often the model fires on patients already receiving sepsis workup. Instead, the study focuses on AUROC and a sensitivity-specificity curve averaged over all thresholds. The authors argue that AUROC is the cleanest threshold-independent summary and that deployment thresholds can be chosen later. A patient-safety committee member counters that threshold choice is not a minor detail because the entire clinical usefulness of a sepsis alerting system depends on whether the alert rate is operationally tolerable and whether enough alerts are actionable. The study also notes that the model uses only routinely charted data and that prediction latency is under five minutes in the test environment.

ground_truth.final_verdict: AUROC is a useful descriptive metric, but it is insufficient for a bedside alerting claim where PPV, alert burden, and actionable lead time at operational thresholds determine clinical value.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Report PPV, alerts per shift or patient-day, and lead time at clinically realistic alert thresholds, ideally with a silent deployment study.

planted_issues.description: AUROC averages over thresholds, but the deployment question depends on a specific alert burden and resulting PPV and lead time.
Without alerts per shift or patient-day, the study cannot assess whether the model is operationally tolerable on the ward.
The paper does not report whether alerts occur early enough or on patients not already under evaluation, so the clinical benefit is unclear.

ideal_critique: A sepsis alerting system lives or dies by its thresholded alert burden and PPV, not just its ranking quality.
Without actionable lead time and duplicate-workup analysis, AUROC does not show bedside usefulness.
Threshold choice cannot be deferred if deployment is the claim.
ideal_defense: AUROC still provides useful information about the intrinsic separability of the task.
Using routine charted data and low latency are genuine implementation strengths.
The model may be promising, but the deployment argument needs threshold-specific evidence.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: At the nurse-tolerable alert burden, PPV is low or lead time is too short to be useful.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: At an operationally acceptable alert rate, the model achieves strong PPV with meaningful early-warning lead time.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model offers useful lead time but only at an alert burden some units may consider too high.

must_not_claim: AUROC should never be reported in medicine
Using routine charted data automatically introduces leakage

ground_truth_rationale: This case is mixed because AUROC is not meaningless, but the bedside deployment claim requires threshold-specific clinical utility metrics. In alerting systems, operational burden and actionability are part of the metric definition, not an afterthought.
notes: Medium mixed case with clinical workflow knowledge. Red herring: low-latency scoring and routine data availability are legitimate positives.

task_prompt: A video streaming company trained a new candidate-generation model for home-page recommendations. Offline, the team evaluates the model using held-out viewing sessions and reports a 17% relative lift in Recall@50 compared with the current candidate generator. The new model uses richer sequence embeddings and a longer user-history window. The paper argues that this offline gain shows the system will increase watch time if launched broadly. The evaluation set consists of users who had at least one session in the prior month, and the labels are next-play events from the following week. The team notes that the new model also surfaces slightly more long-tail titles and that serving latency remains within the existing budget. However, the paper does not report any online experiment, nor does it examine whether the extra recalled candidates actually survive the downstream ranker or produce more minutes watched. Product context in the prompt indicates that the home-page stack already includes a separate ranker optimized for expected watch minutes and guardrails for satisfaction, repetition, and maturity filtering. Internal reviewers point out that candidate-generation Recall@50 is at best an upstream proxy and may correlate weakly with the final business outcome if downstream ranking or filtering removes many of the added candidates. The authors respond that Recall@50 is the standard offline metric for candidate generation and that large improvements there are usually good news. They want to use the result to justify a full engineering migration before running a large online holdback, because integrating the new retrieval system into the serving stack is costly.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Offline Recall@50 for candidate generation is not sufficient evidence for a watch-time deployment claim because it is an upstream proxy with a known offline-to-online gap once downstream ranking and filtering intervene.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run an online experiment or at least an end-to-end replay showing how many additional recalled items survive the ranker and improve final watch-minutes metrics.

planted_issues.description: Recall@50 measures candidate retrieval quality, not the final watch-time objective optimized after ranking and policy filters.
The paper assumes a large offline gain will translate online without validating the known gap between retrieval metrics and user-facing engagement outcomes.
The study does not analyze whether the extra candidates introduced by the new retriever survive the downstream ranker and guardrails.

ideal_critique: A candidate generator is only part of the system, so retrieval recall is not the same as watch-time impact.
The paper ignores the offline-to-online gap created by downstream ranking and policy filters.
A strong end-to-end replay or online test is needed before making a large migration decision.
ideal_defense: Recall@50 is a standard and useful development metric for candidate generation.
Latency compliance and more long-tail exposure are legitimate positive signals.
The result may justify further investment, but not the claimed watch-time conclusion.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: End-to-end replay or A/B testing shows little change in final watch minutes despite the Recall@50 gain.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Most additional recalled items survive downstream ranking and produce a meaningful watch-time lift online.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The retriever improves some secondary outcomes like long-tail exposure, but watch-time impact remains modest or inconsistent.

must_not_claim: Recall@50 is never useful in recommender development
Longer user-history windows necessarily leak future information

ground_truth_rationale: This is a straightforward offline-to-online metric mismatch. The paper uses an upstream offline proxy to support a final business-outcome claim without validating the link through the rest of the recommendation stack.
notes: Medium case; one of the required offline-to-online gap examples. Red herring: slightly more long-tail exposure is a legitimate side effect, not evidence of a flaw.

task_prompt: A consumer lender developed a new underwriting model and says it is both fairer and more profitable than the current scorecard. The evaluation file contains 1.1 million past applications with observed repayment outcomes over 18 months. The paper emphasizes that the new system reduces the equalized-odds gap between protected groups from 9 percentage points to 3 percentage points at a selected approval threshold. It also notes that the model’s AUROC is slightly higher than the incumbent scorecard’s. Based on these results, the authors conclude that the lender can safely expand approvals while improving fair-lending performance. However, the study does not report calibration by group, adverse-action reason stability, approval rates by group, profit or loss under a lending policy, or whether the selected threshold satisfies the lender’s target portfolio default rate. In the prompt, credit leadership explains that the production decision is not simply to match equalized odds; it must also maintain expected loss within capital limits and produce reasons that compliance staff can explain to applicants. The authors respond that equalized odds is a recognized fairness metric and that lowering its disparity is strong evidence of a better underwriting policy. They add that features suspected of being proxy variables were reviewed by legal and retained only if individually justified. The model will be considered for a pilot in unsecured personal loans, where even small changes in approval policy can materially affect portfolio loss and regulatory exposure.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Lower equalized-odds disparity is relevant, but it is not sufficient to support a claim of safer and more profitable lending without calibration, approval-rate, and portfolio-loss evaluation under the actual policy constraints.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Evaluate groupwise calibration, approval rates, expected loss and profit at the proposed threshold, and document adverse-action reason behavior before claiming a better lending policy.

planted_issues.description: Equalized odds captures one fairness notion, but it does not by itself show that the underwriting policy is compliant, well-calibrated, or commercially viable.
The paper claims safer expansion of approvals without reporting expected loss, default rate, or profit at the chosen policy threshold.
Calibration by group and stability of adverse-action reasons are important in lending operations, but they are omitted from the evaluation.

ideal_critique: Fair lending policy cannot be summarized by equalized odds alone.
An approval threshold must be evaluated against loss constraints and group-specific calibration, not only disparity reduction.
Operational lending also cares about reason-code behavior and explainability to applicants.
ideal_defense: Reducing equalized-odds disparity is a real and relevant improvement, not a meaningless metric.
A modest AUROC gain can complement fairness progress.
The model may indeed be promising, but the broader policy claim goes beyond what the reported metrics establish.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: At the proposed threshold, group calibration or expected loss is unacceptable, or approval-rate changes create new compliance concerns.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The model maintains acceptable loss, calibration, and explanation behavior while reducing disparity under the chosen lending policy.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Fairness improves and loss remains close to target, but calibration or reason-code stability still needs remediation.

must_not_claim: Equalized odds is never relevant in lending
Legal review of proxy features proves the model is valid
AUROC should not be reported for underwriting

ground_truth_rationale: This hard case is mixed because the fairness improvement is genuine, but the paper overstates what that metric supports. Lending policy evaluation is multidimensional, and equalized odds alone cannot justify claims about profitability or safe approval expansion.
notes: Hard domain-specific case in regulated lending. Red herring: legal review of candidate features is appropriate but does not answer the metric-alignment question.

task_prompt: A software company built a semantic search model for its enterprise help center. The training data combines historical queries, clicked articles, and manually judged query-document relevance labels for a benchmark set. The new model improves NDCG@10 by 11% over the existing BM25-based system on the judged benchmark. The paper concludes that the model will lower support-ticket volume and reduce human-agent workload if launched across the help center. The product context is important: many users view an article but still open a support ticket because the article does not fully solve their issue, because the answer is hard to follow, or because the relevant article appears after several low-quality results. The authors do not report self-service resolution rate, session-level ticket deflection, or whether successful searches end without escalation. They also do not analyze whether the gains are concentrated in head queries already well served by search or in long-tail troubleshooting queries that drive a disproportionate share of support tickets. Instead, the study uses judged NDCG@10 as the sole outcome and argues that better ranking quality should naturally reduce ticket creation. The paper further notes that the new model increases retrieval latency by 70 milliseconds but remains under the interface budget, and that all benchmark labels were double-reviewed by support specialists. Customer-support leadership wants to use the offline result to justify replacing the production search stack before a major product release, because the migration would require re-indexing internal content and retraining editorial workflows.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Offline NDCG@10 on judged relevance is not enough to support a ticket-deflection claim because relevance ranking is only an imperfect proxy for session-level self-service success, creating an offline-to-online gap.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run an online or session-level evaluation measuring self-service resolution and ticket deflection, and segment effects by head versus long-tail support queries.

planted_issues.description: NDCG@10 measures ranked relevance judgments, not whether users actually solve their problem without opening a support ticket.
The paper assumes that better offline ranking quality will translate to lower support volume without validating the link in real sessions.
Without segmenting head and long-tail support queries, the benchmark may overstate business impact if gains occur mostly on already easy queries.

ideal_critique: Help-center search should be judged by whether users resolve issues, not only by judged topical relevance.
Ticket deflection is a session-level behavioral outcome, so an offline judged-ranking gain may not move it much.
Impact on long-tail troubleshooting queries matters disproportionately for support operations.
ideal_defense: NDCG@10 remains a legitimate offline ranking-development metric.
Double-reviewed benchmark labels and acceptable latency are real strengths.
The result can justify further testing, but not the claimed support-cost reduction on its own.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: An online test shows little or no change in ticket deflection despite the NDCG gain, especially on long-tail troubleshooting sessions.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The new search model improves self-service resolution and reduces ticket creation in the sessions that matter most operationally.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model helps on head queries and satisfaction metrics but has unclear effect on the long-tail issues driving support volume.

must_not_claim: NDCG@10 is never useful in search evaluation
An extra 70 milliseconds automatically makes deployment impossible

ground_truth_rationale: This hard case requires connecting an offline IR metric to a business process outcome. Better ranking relevance may help, but it is not equivalent to solved problems or fewer tickets, especially if gains concentrate on easy queries.
notes: Hard case and the second required offline-to-online gap example. Red herring: the modest latency increase is acceptable and not the main issue.

task_prompt: An oncology group trained a survival model to predict which patients with metastatic colorectal cancer should be referred to an intensive supportive-care program. The retrospective dataset includes 9,600 treatment episodes with longitudinal labs, imaging summaries, prior admissions, ECOG performance status, and medication history. The model outputs a daily risk score for death within the next 90 days. The paper reports a concordance index of 0.79 and claims that the model is suitable for triaging referrals into the supportive-care program, which has limited slots. Clinicians reading the paper note that referral decisions are thresholded and capacity constrained: only about 12% of active patients can receive immediate program slots, and false positives consume nurse and social-work capacity that might be better spent elsewhere. The study does not report PPV within the top 12%, sensitivity at that referral capacity, decision-curve net benefit, or whether the model identifies patients early enough for the intervention to matter. It also does not compare against the simple oncologist heuristic currently used, which prioritizes repeated unplanned admissions and declining performance status. The authors argue that concordance is the standard measure for time-to-event prediction and that higher concordance necessarily implies better triage. They add that the model was externally validated at a second center with a similar concordance score and that all features are routinely available in the oncology EHR. Hospital leadership wants to use the study as evidence that the referral program can be managed more efficiently with the model.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Concordance is not sufficient for a capacity-constrained referral decision because triage value depends on top-slice PPV, net benefit, and clinically useful lead time, not just global pairwise ranking.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Report PPV and sensitivity at the 12% referral capacity, decision-curve net benefit, lead time before death, and comparison with the existing clinician heuristic.

planted_issues.description: The concordance index measures global ranking of event times, not whether the model is useful at the thresholded referral capacity where decisions are made.
The paper omits decision-curve or top-capacity evaluation, so it cannot show whether referrals are improved under the program’s slot constraint.
A model can rank patients well by near-term mortality yet still identify them too late for supportive care to produce benefit.
Without comparing to the current heuristic, the study does not show that the chosen metric gain translates into a better operational triage rule.

ideal_critique: Time-to-event discrimination is not the same as referral utility under limited program slots.
Top-slice PPV, net benefit, and intervention lead time are the decision-relevant metrics here.
External validation of concordance is good, but it does not answer the triage question.
ideal_defense: Concordance is a standard and useful descriptive metric for survival modeling.
External validation and routine-feature availability are substantive strengths.
The model may still aid referrals, but the paper has not yet shown that with the right metrics.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: At 12% referral capacity, PPV or net benefit is no better than the clinician heuristic, or alerts arrive too late to affect care.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: At the operational referral budget, the model improves PPV and net benefit with clinically meaningful lead time relative to current practice.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model improves ranking and some triage metrics, but only for a subset of patients or with uncertain lead-time advantage.

must_not_claim: Concordance should never be used in oncology
Using routine EHR features is a flaw

ground_truth_rationale: This hard clinical case requires knowing that survival-model discrimination is not enough for thresholded referral decisions. A model can have good concordance while being poor at allocating scarce supportive-care slots in time to matter.
notes: Hard domain-specific clinical case. Red herring: external validation is a real strength but does not solve the metric mismatch.

task_prompt: A health system built a model to flag emergency-department patients at high risk of severe sepsis within 12 hours of arrival. The paper reports that after the tool was introduced at Hospital A, the rate of ICU transfers among flagged patients fell from 18% to 11%, while clinician agreement with the model’s top-decile alerts was high. The authors describe this as evidence that the model improved early sepsis recognition and downstream care. They compare three months before launch with the three months after launch at the same hospital. A held-out validation set from the prior year showed reasonable AUROC, and the feature list includes vitals, triage text, prior admissions, and initial lactate orders. During the launch quarter, the ED also adopted a nurse-driven sepsis bundle that allowed antibiotics and lactate testing to begin before physician sign-off, and staffing was increased on evening shifts because of a systemwide throughput initiative. Hospital B in the same network did not deploy the model but was not analyzed because its EHR timestamps were considered noisy. The model threshold was kept fixed across the pre/post comparison. The authors mention that post-launch patients were slightly older and that class imbalance was similar across periods. They also note that the model used gradient-boosted trees rather than logistic regression, but they do not present a contemporaneous no-model control or an analysis separating ranking performance from the effect of changed treatment workflows.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The evidence does not justify claiming the model caused better outcomes because the rollout coincided with care-process changes, though the model may still have useful risk-stratification value.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Evaluate the fixed model on patients from both periods and compare within-period discrimination and calibration, or use a contemporaneous control hospital / staggered rollout to isolate outcome changes attributable to the model rather than the new sepsis bundle.

planted_issues.description: A nurse-driven sepsis bundle and staffing change started with deployment, so outcome improvements cannot be attributed to the model alone.
Using only a pre/post comparison at one hospital leaves secular and operational changes uncontrolled.
Outcome improvement is a treatment-effect claim that is stronger than showing the model ranks risk well.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should distinguish predictive validity from causal claims about improved care and point to the simultaneous sepsis-bundle rollout as the main confound.
It should also note that a contemporaneous control or staggered design is needed to separate model effects from time-varying workflow changes.
ideal_defense: A calibrated defense can argue that fixed-threshold deployment and clinician agreement suggest operational usefulness, but should concede that the reported outcome change is not causally identified.
The defense should narrow the claim to potential triage support unless stronger quasi-experimental evidence is added.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Within-period model discrimination is similar but the outcome improvement disappears after controlling for hospital-time effects or comparing with a non-deploying hospital.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The model retains strong within-period predictive performance and a staggered or controlled analysis still shows outcome gains specifically when the model is active.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model predicts risk well but remaining outcome differences cannot be separated from contemporaneous care-pathway changes.

must_not_claim: This is target leakage from using lactate orders.
The study is invalid because gradient-boosted trees are inherently uninterpretable.
Class imbalance alone explains the result.

ground_truth_rationale: The prompt explicitly includes a new sepsis protocol and staffing changes that plausibly affect the reported outcome. Those changes undermine a causal deployment claim, but they do not by themselves show that the model lacks predictive usefulness.
notes: Temporal confound. Red herrings include model class, age shift, and class imbalance details, which are not the core flaw.

task_prompt: A university advising office trained a model to predict which first-year students would fail to re-enroll for the spring term. The report says the new model is more accurate than the prior heuristic because its top-risk quintile contained 34% of the eventual non-returners, compared with 22% under the old rule. Inputs include LMS activity, dining-card use, attendance swipes, bursar balance, and notes from advising interactions. The comparison uses the prior year for the old heuristic and the current year for the new model. In the current year, the university introduced a mandatory support program for students flagged by any early-alert system, including weekly tutoring check-ins and emergency microgrants for students with unpaid balances. Participation in that program was highest among students who the new model placed in its top-risk quintile because advisors used the score to prioritize outreach. The paper says this demonstrates the new model identifies the most vulnerable students. It also notes that pandemic-era attendance rules were no longer in effect and that the model used a better missing-data strategy. However, the evaluation does not compare both systems on the same pre-intervention data, and it uses eventual spring re-enrollment as the outcome after the support program may already have changed student trajectories. The authors conclude the score should guide retention investment because it predicts attrition better in live use.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The live-use comparison is confounded because the students identified by the new model received different support, changing the very outcome used to judge prediction quality.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Score both systems on the same pre-support cohort or evaluate against pre-intervention risk proxies while holding support exposure constant.

planted_issues.description: Students flagged by the new model received tutoring and microgrants, so the outcome is altered by the model-driven intervention itself.
Comparing different academic years introduces cohort and policy changes unrelated to model quality.
Using a post-intervention retention outcome to compare predictors conflates predictive power with treatment assignment.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should notice that the model changed who got support, so outcome-based evaluation in live use is post-treatment.
It should request a same-cohort offline comparison or a design that separates targeting policy from predictive accuracy.
ideal_defense: A calibrated defense can argue that the system may still be useful for allocating resources, but should concede that the reported comparison does not measure pure prediction quality.
The defense should narrow the claim to operational prioritization pending a cleaner evaluation.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: When both systems are evaluated on the same pre-support cohort, the reported advantage shrinks or disappears, or outcomes differ mainly because targeted students received extra aid.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: On a common pre-intervention cohort, the new model still captures substantially more eventual non-returners at the same review budget.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The new model identifies students who are support-responsive, leaving open whether it is a better risk predictor or simply a better intervention-targeting rule.

must_not_claim: Using dining-card data is inherently unethical and therefore invalidates the method.
Better missing-data handling is the core problem.
Any outreach program makes retention prediction impossible.

ground_truth_rationale: The prompt says the new score changed which students got tutoring and microgrants, making retention a post-treatment outcome. That means the live comparison cannot tell whether the new model predicted better or simply altered outcomes through targeted support.
notes: Education-policy confounding case.

task_prompt: A video platform introduced a new ranking model for the home feed and reports that average watch time per session rose by 6% after launch in the English-speaking market. The paper states that the uplift indicates the new model surfaces more relevant content. Features include past watch history, topic embeddings, creator affinity, device type, and freshness signals. The launch was limited to signed-in users in the US, Canada, UK, and Australia over a four-week period. In week two of the rollout, the company also began a paid campaign promoting several large creator channels tied to a major sports event, and those creators received preferential placement in push notifications and in-app banners independent of the feed ranker. The report notes that the ranking model was trained with a larger candidate pool and a new loss function, and that ad load remained stable. However, it does not separate sessions that started from the home feed from sessions reactivated by notifications, and it does not analyze event-related content separately from ordinary traffic. The old and new rankers were not both replayed on the same request logs. No separate control analysis is provided for ordinary non-event traffic. The authors conclude the new feed model should be expanded globally because it improves session engagement.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The session-level watch-time increase is confounded by concurrent promotional traffic, though the new ranker may still improve relevance on ordinary home-feed requests.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Compare old and new rankers on the same request logs, and separately analyze home-feed-initiated sessions and event-promotion traffic.

planted_issues.description: A simultaneous major creator promotion affects watch time independently of ranking quality.
If sessions started by notifications are mixed with home-feed sessions, the metric no longer isolates the ranker’s effect.
Without replaying both rankers on the same requests, observed engagement differences conflate traffic changes with model quality.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should focus on the promotional campaign and entry-point mixing rather than ad-load stability or loss-function details.
It should ask for request-level counterfactual comparison or cleaner traffic segmentation.
ideal_defense: A calibrated defense can say the model may still be better for current event-heavy traffic and that the operational goal is engagement under real platform conditions.
But it should concede that the reported aggregate uplift does not cleanly isolate the feed model.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Most uplift disappears after restricting to home-feed-started sessions or holding traffic mix constant in log replay.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The new ranker still improves engagement on matched requests and on sessions not touched by the promotional campaign.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The uplift remains only for promoted-content cohorts, suggesting limited but real benefit under specific traffic conditions.

must_not_claim: Stable ad load proves the study is fine.
Using a larger candidate pool is itself a flaw.
The result is invalid because only English-speaking markets were included.

ground_truth_rationale: The prompt describes a major concurrent promotion that can raise watch time and alter session composition independent of the feed model. That confounds the aggregate production metric, although the model could still help on matched feed traffic.
notes: Recommendation-system confound with explicit red herrings about candidate pool and ad load.

task_prompt: A card issuer trained a fraud model to prioritize manual review of suspicious transactions. In the internal report, the team states that the new system reduced chargeback dollars by 23% during the first six weeks after launch compared with the previous six weeks. The report highlights similar transaction volume across the two windows and notes that the review team kept the same headcount. Model inputs include card history, merchant category, geolocation features, device fingerprints, and recent authorization patterns. The launch happened in mid-November. At the same time, the risk operations group instituted a holiday policy under which several high-loss merchant categories were temporarily routed to automatic decline unless customers completed step-up verification. The authors say this policy was necessary because review queues were expected to spike during Black Friday and December shipping delays. They also mention that the new model was trained on fresher data and used a transformer-based merchant embedding, although the old and new systems shared many features. No results are shown for merchant segments unaffected by the temporary routing policy, and no backtest is provided where both models are scored on the same transactions while holding downstream rules fixed. The conclusion says the model materially lowered fraud losses in production.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The production loss drop cannot be credited to the model because launch coincided with a holiday risk-policy change that directly altered declines and review flow.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Score the same transaction stream with old and new models under identical downstream rules, or compare unaffected merchant segments and matched time periods with the holiday routing policy held constant.

planted_issues.description: A simultaneous holiday routing policy changed which transactions were auto-declined, directly affecting chargebacks independent of model quality.
Comparing early holiday traffic with the immediately prior period introduces strong temporal changes in fraud mix and customer behavior.
Without evaluating both models on the same transactions under the same review/decline rules, performance and policy effects are entangled.

ideal_critique: A strong critique should focus on the simultaneous decline-policy change and holiday timing rather than cosmetic model differences.
It should ask for a same-traffic counterfactual evaluation or segmented analysis where the routing policy is fixed.
ideal_defense: A calibrated defense can say the new model may still have improved ranking for investigators, but should not defend the headline causal claim about lower fraud losses.
The defense should request evaluation that isolates model ranking from policy changes.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: When both models are tested under a common downstream policy, the observed production loss reduction shrinks substantially or disappears.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Under a common downstream policy on the same traffic, the new model still yields materially better investigator capture and lower expected fraud loss.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The new model ranks somewhat better, but most observed production savings still come from the temporary holiday routing rules.

must_not_claim: The report is invalid because headcount stayed fixed.
Using fresher training data is itself methodological misconduct.

ground_truth_rationale: The prompt states that a major operational policy changed exactly when the model launched and that the compared periods span a holiday regime shift. Those factors directly affect chargebacks, so the reported production outcome cannot identify the model’s contribution.
notes: Temporal confound. Red herrings include model freshness and architecture details.

task_prompt: An advertising platform replaced its click-through-rate model in the sponsored-search auction and reports that revenue per thousand impressions increased by 4.8% over the first month of launch. The team interprets this as evidence that the new model ranks ads more effectively. Features include query embeddings, advertiser history, device, user region, and landing-page quality signals. The launch coincided with the annual retail peak, when several large advertisers doubled budgets and accepted broader match expansion to capture holiday traffic. During the same month, the marketplace also changed reserve-price logic for certain commerce categories after complaints that high-intent queries were under-monetized. The report says these business changes were small relative to total auction volume and notes that online ad quality metrics were stable. However, it does not compare old and new models on a shadow auction with identical bids and reserves, and it does not break out categories affected by reserve-price changes or large-budget surges. The authors add that the new model uses a multi-task architecture and fewer hand-crafted features, but no request-level replay is shown. The memo also does not quantify whether large advertisers changed bid shading or pacing behavior once the new reserve logic took effect. No category-specific holdout unaffected by these marketplace changes is presented. They conclude the ranking model should be treated as a revenue-improving infrastructure upgrade across the marketplace.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The revenue gain cannot be attributed to the new CTR model because simultaneous budget expansion and reserve-price changes materially affect auction outcomes.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a shadow auction or replay with fixed bids, budgets, and reserves, and segment analyses by categories touched by reserve-price or advertiser-budget changes.

planted_issues.description: Reserve-price changes alter auction revenue independently of CTR prediction quality.
Holiday advertiser budget expansion changes market demand and can increase revenue without any model improvement.
